textPlain: > Here’s the problem for consumers: To meet data center demand, utilities are building new power plants and power lines that are needed only because of data center growth. If state regulators allow utilities to follow the standard approach of splitting the costs of new infrastructure among all consumers, the public will end up paying to supply data centers with all that power.I don’t agree with the premise of this article. Of course they will “include these costs” for all ratepayers because the alternative would be Meta (or whomever) just buys all the electricity (or at least a much greater share). Of course ratepayers share in the cost of building new infra because the equilibrium price of electricity has gone up.
Google and Meta offer AI usage for free so there is a public benefit in return that people are getting.
I breathe in oxygen and breathe out carbon dioxide for free, maybe Google and Meta can give me a billion dollars for the public benefit I do.
I'm sure the people that won't be able to afford to heat their homes will be thankful they'll be able to generate limited amounts of slop for free faster than ever before
